text: The surgeon begins the robotic myotomy and fundoplication by dissecting the gastrohepatic omentum to expose the right crus of the diaphragm. The phrenoesophageal membrane is then dissected, followed by the separation of the left crus of the diaphragm from the stomach.

The surgeon transects the gastrophenic ligament and creates a window in the retrogastric space for Penrose drain placement. A myotomy is initiated at the distal gastroesophageal junction by lifting muscle fibers, creating a space between the longitudinal circular muscle fibers and the stomach mucosa. The myotomy is extended onto the esophagus in small bites, using the bulging mucosa as a visual guide.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully transect muscle fibers at the gastroesophageal junction, ensuring the scalpel tip is cooled periodically to prevent mucosal injury. Muscle fibers are stretched and lifted off the mucosa, with small bites taken to complete the myotomy. The myotomy is extended into the mediastinum while preserving the anterior vagus nerve. A measuring ruler is used to confirm the myotomy length of seven to eight centimeters.

The surgeon begins the door fundoplication by taking an initial stitch from the stomach to the left crus and the divided muscle fibers of the myotomy on the left side. A second suture is placed from the stomach to the muscle fibers of the myotomy, ensuring the myotomy remains open and the muscle fibers are kept apart.

The surgeon places a third suture from the stomach to the left muscle fibers, completing the left side of the door fundoplication. The stomach is then rolled onto the right side over the esophagus. An initial stitch is placed from the stomach to the right muscle fibers and right cruz, followed by a second stitch from the stomach to the right muscle fibers at the myotomy site. A third suture is placed from the stomach to the previously divided muscle fibers on the right aspect of the myotomy, completing the robotic color myotomy and door fundoplication.
